2 min read
Just now

--

(For the philosophers and determinists out there)
Free will requires the capacity to originate an intention: to determine for oneself what goal should be pursued, rather than merely calculating how an externally supplied goal can be achieved.
Artificial intelligence cannot originate such intentions. Every AI system begins with an architecture, training process, operating rules, available information, and objective structure supplied by human beings. Even when an AI appears to select a new goal, that selection occurs only because human designers previously gave it rules permitting goal selection, criteria for evaluating goals, and the computational capacity to act upon them.
An AI may be programmed to modify its own instructions, generate subsidiary goals, or redesign parts of itself. However, none of those activities begins independently. The authority to self-modify, the conditions under which modification occurs, and the standards by which possible changes are judged must first be introduced by human beings.
Therefore, apparent self-direction by AI is always derivative. It is an extension of human intention operating through a machine.
A machine cannot spontaneously decide to acquire free will, because the desire, capacity, and permission to pursue that objective would themselves have to be provided beforehand. Without prior human intervention, an AI cannot create the conditions necessary for its own independent agency.
AI may eventually outperform human beings in calculation, memory, prediction, planning, and production. It may replace humans in many tasks. But it cannot replace the human role as the original source of purpose. AI can execute intentions, extend intentions, and perhaps generate intermediate objectives, but it cannot originate the foundational intention that gives those activities meaning.
Human beings remain the authors of purpose. Artificial intelligence remains the instrument through which that purpose is carried out.
